system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
## Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a 2M-user health app launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy.** It resolves the central tension of this product better than any alternative: it delivers full clinical-grade capability while making a structurally credible privacy promise. In health, both halves of that sentence are non-negotiable.

**Why capability matters here.** A health coach that is 30-40% weaker (Option A's local-only ceiling) is not a privacy-respecting product — it's a worse product that happens to be private. Users with real health questions deserve accurate guidance, and a degraded model risks giving unsafe or unhelpful advice. The capability gap in Option A and the research-immaturity of Option E (federated learning's reliability is still "under active research") make them unsuitable for a high-stakes domain at this scale today. You don't experiment with deployment architecture on 2M people's health data.

**Why the privacy posture must be structural, not policy-based.** Options C and D rely on training-consent mechanics. Option C's opt-out default is a regulatory liability — GDPR, the emerging health-data regimes, and several state laws treat default use of sensitive health data for model training as a grey area at best, an enforcement target at worst. For a consumer health brand, one adverse ruling or breach headline is existential. Option D is more defensible but still ties the product's value proposition to harvesting user data, which keeps trust contingent on consent UX rather than architecture.

**Why B wins the trust axis.** "Your health data is never used to train any model, and is only ever decrypted inside a secure enclave" is a promise you can make plainly, market on, and verify with audits. It removes the entire category of training-consent regulatory exposure. Trust is this company's core asset; B protects it by design rather than by disclosure.

**The cost objection is the weakest one.** The ~3x infrastructure cost is real but bounded, quantifiable, and falls as confidential-computing hardware matures. Weigh that against the unbounded, tail-risk costs of C/D: regulatory penalties, breach liability, and reputational collapse. Paying a premium to take an entire risk class off the table is sound insurance, not waste.

**Path forward:** Launch on B. Keep federated learning (E) as an R&D track to potentially reduce costs later — but only once its reliability is proven, never as the launch bet.
